text:
MEDICAL ONCOLOGY FOLLOW-UP NOTE
Gynecologic Oncology / Medical Oncology

PATIENT: Margaret Osei-Bonsu
DIAGNOSIS: High-grade serous carcinoma of bilateral ovaries and fallopian tubes, FIGO stage IIIC (pT3c pN1 M0), germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant (c.5266dupC)
CURRENT REGIMEN: Niraparib (Zejula) 100 mg orally once daily — maintenance therapy (dose-reduced from 200 mg following Grade 3 thrombocytopenia; currently on reduced dose for approximately 8 weeks)

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT / REASON FOR VISIT:
Routine follow-up visit for assessment of niraparib tolerability and CBC monitoring, approximately 8 weeks following dose reduction from 200 mg to 100 mg daily. Patient also reporting blood pressure concern flagged at last visit.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Ms. Osei-Bonsu is a 47-year-old Ghanaian-American woman with a diagnosis of FIGO IIIC high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma made approximately 10–11 months ago. Her disease course to date has been notable for successful primary debulking surgery achieving complete gross resection (R0), followed by six cycles of adjuvant carboplatin/paclitaxel chemotherapy complicated by a carboplatin hypersensitivity reaction (managed with desensitization protocol) and cumulative paclitaxel-related peripheral neuropathy requiring dose reduction in cycles 5 and 6. She achieved a complete biochemical and radiographic response following chemotherapy, with CA-125 normalizing to 11 U/mL and post-treatment CT showing no evidence of disease.

She was subsequently initiated on niraparib (Zejula) maintenance therapy at 200 mg orally once daily per individualized dosing guidance (weight 64 kg, baseline platelets within acceptable range). Approximately three weeks into maintenance therapy, her weekly CBC monitoring revealed Grade 3 thrombocytopenia with a nadir platelet count of 58,000/uL. She was completely asymptomatic — no bruising, petechiae, epistaxis, or bleeding at any point. Hematology was consulted; peripheral smear showed no microangiopathic changes, no schistocytes, and no features suggestive of ITP or TTP. The thrombocytopenia was attributed to niraparib-induced myelosuppression, a well-recognized and class-related adverse effect of PARP inhibitors. Niraparib was held until platelets recovered to 118,000/uL at week 5 of the hold, and the drug was then restarted at the dose-reduced level of 100 mg daily.

She presents today, approximately 8 weeks following the dose reduction to 100 mg daily, for reassessment. She reports that she has been taking the medication consistently at bedtime as instructed and has not missed any doses. She reports mild nausea occurring occasionally in the morning, which she manages with PRN ondansetron (using approximately 2–3 times per week). No vomiting. She denies any bruising, petechiae, or unusual bleeding. She reports mild fatigue, which she rates as 2–3/10 in severity — she attributes this partly to niraparib and partly to lingering post-chemotherapy deconditioning. She is also reporting mild intermittent headaches, occurring approximately 3–4 times per week, typically frontal in distribution, non-pulsatile, and without associated visual changes, nausea, phonophobia, or photophobia. She notes that the headaches improved somewhat after she began monitoring her blood pressure at home following a recommendation from her primary care physician (Dr. [PCP name not on file]). Home blood pressure readings have ranged from 134–146 systolic over the past several weeks. She is currently not taking any antihypertensive medication.

Peripheral neuropathy: She describes mild persistent tingling in the bilateral fingertips and toes, which she rates as stable and improved compared to the nadir during chemotherapy. She reports no functional limitations from the neuropathy. She is continuing duloxetine 30 mg daily, which she feels is helping modestly.

She has returned to part-time work as a mathematics teacher and reports that her energy is sufficient for classroom duties. She is not yet back to full-time status due to fatigue. She remains engaged with the oncology support group and reports that her mood and emotional coping have improved substantially. She denies depression or anxiety at the level she experienced at diagnosis. She is sleeping reasonably well.

No fever, chills, night sweats, chest pain, shortness of breath, abdominal pain, or change in bowel or bladder habits. No pelvic pressure or bloating. No new lymphadenopathy detected by self-examination.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
- Constitutional: Mild fatigue (Grade 1–2), no fever or chills
- HEENT: Mild intermittent frontal headaches as above; no visual disturbance, no epistaxis
- Cardiovascular: No chest pain or palpitations; elevated home blood pressure readings as noted
- Pulmonary: No dyspnea, cough, or hemoptysis
- GI: Mild intermittent nausea (Grade 1), no vomiting, no abdominal pain, no change in bowel habits; appetite near-normal
- GU: No hematuria, no dysuria, no urinary frequency; no new pelvic symptoms
- MSK: No new bone pain or arthralgias
- Neurological: Persistent mild bilateral distal sensory neuropathy (Grade 1), stable; mild intermittent headaches as above
- Skin/hematologic: No petechiae, no ecchymoses, no easy bruising, no bleeding gums; scalp hair beginning to regrow (Grade 1 alopecia by patient report, noticeable regrowth in progress)
- Psychiatric: Improved mood; ongoing participation in support group; no suicidal ideation

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:
General: Well-appearing, well-nourished woman in no acute distress. Presents in casual street clothes. Short, fine hair regrowth evident on scalp bilaterally. Alert and interactive.
Vital Signs: Blood pressure 138/86 mmHg (right arm, seated, repeated after 5 minutes: 140/88 mmHg); heart rate 78 bpm and regular; respiratory rate 14 breaths/min; temperature 36.8°C; O2 saturation 98% on room air; weight 63.4 kg (baseline 64 kg, stable).
HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic. No scleral icterus. Oropharynx clear, no mucositis. No oral petechiae or bleeding.
Neck: No cervical, supraclavicular, or submandibular lymphadenopathy palpated.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm; normal S1 and S2; no murmurs, rubs, or gallops.
Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally; no dullness to percussion; no accessory muscle use.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. Well-healed midline laparotomy scar without evidence of dehiscence, hernia, or induration. No palpable organomegaly. No ascites by percussion or palpation. No pelvic masses palpable on external exam.
Extremities: No lower extremity edema. No petechiae or ecchymoses on the skin of the arms or legs.
Neurological: Alert and oriented. Cranial nerves II–XII intact. Diminished light touch sensation to the bilateral fingertips and toes on testing with monofilament, consistent with Grade 1 peripheral sensory neuropathy — stable compared with prior visit. Strength 5/5 in all four extremities. Coordination intact. Gait normal.
Skin: No petechiae, purpura, or bruising. Scalp hair re-growing, estimated Grade 1 alopecia recovery in progress. No rashes.
Lymph nodes: No axillary, inguinal, or popliteal lymphadenopathy.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS (obtained today):
Complete Blood Count:
  - WBC: 5.1 x10^9/L (reference 4.0–10.5) — within normal limits
  - Absolute neutrophil count: 3.1 x10^9/L — normal
  - Hemoglobin: 11.4 g/dL (reference 12.0–16.0) — Grade 1 anemia (mild, stable)
  - Hematocrit: 34.2%
  - MCV: 87 fL (normocytic)
  - Platelets: 142,000/uL (reference 150,000–400,000) — Grade 1 thrombocytopenia; notably improved from nadir of 58,000/uL; trending upward since dose reduction

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
  - Sodium: 139 mEq/L; Potassium: 4.1 mEq/L; Chloride: 103 mEq/L; Bicarbonate: 24 mEq/L
  - BUN: 14 mg/dL; Creatinine: 0.81 mg/dL; eGFR: >60 mL/min/1.73m²
  - Glucose: 94 mg/dL
  - Total bilirubin: 0.6 mg/dL; Direct bilirubin: 0.2 mg/dL
  - AST: 22 U/L; ALT: 19 U/L; Alkaline phosphatase: 67 U/L
  - Total protein: 7.1 g/dL; Albumin: 3.9 g/dL

Tumor Markers:
  - CA-125: 9 U/mL (within normal range; <35 U/mL) — sustained normal; down from 3,847 U/mL at diagnosis
  - HE4: 46 pmol/L (normal)

Urinalysis: Yellow, clear; pH 6.0; no blood, no protein, no nitrites, no leukocyte esterase. Normal.

---

IMAGING:
No new imaging obtained at today's visit. Most recent CT chest/abdomen/pelvis (obtained approximately 4 weeks after completion of cycle 6 chemotherapy) demonstrated no evidence of disease — complete radiographic response. Repeat surveillance imaging planned per protocol at 12 months from chemotherapy completion.

---

ASSESSMENT:

1. High-grade serous ovarian carcinoma, FIGO IIIC, germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant (c.5266dupC) — currently in complete biochemical and radiographic remission following R0 resection and 6 cycles of carboplatin/paclitaxel. CA-125 today 9 U/mL, normalized and stable. No clinical, laboratory, or imaging evidence of disease recurrence at this time.

2. Niraparib (Zejula) maintenance therapy — dose-reduced to 100 mg daily following Grade 3 thrombocytopenia approximately 3 weeks after initiation at 200 mg. Patient is now 8 weeks into the reduced dose. Platelets have recovered substantially to 142,000/uL today (from nadir of 58,000/uL), consistent with appropriate hematologic recovery at the lower dose. This is an encouraging trend. Grade 1 thrombocytopenia at 100 mg is acceptable and does not require further dose reduction per prescribing guidance. Niraparib-associated myelosuppression is a well-characterized class effect of PARP inhibitors and is managed effectively through dose individualization. She is tolerating 100 mg without any bleeding complications.

3. Niraparib-associated hypertension — blood pressure readings today are 138–140/86–88 mmHg on two measurements, consistent with Stage 2 hypertension. Home readings have been persistently elevated. She is also reporting intermittent frontal headaches, which are likely related to the blood pressure elevation. Niraparib is known to cause hypertension in approximately 20% of patients; this is likely a drug effect in the setting of an otherwise healthy, non-hypertensive woman. Initiating antihypertensive therapy is indicated. After discussion with the patient and with coordination of her primary care physician, amlodipine 5 mg orally once daily will be started today. Blood pressure to be rechecked in 2–4 weeks; titration may be needed.

4. Grade 1 peripheral sensory neuropathy, bilateral distal — residual from cumulative paclitaxel exposure. Stable and slowly improving. Duloxetine 30 mg daily continued. No functional impairment. Will continue to monitor at each visit.

5. Grade 1 anemia — Hemoglobin 11.4 g/dL, stable, mild. No transfusion indicated. Likely multifactorial (residual post-chemotherapy marrow recovery and mild PARP inhibitor contribution). Supplemental iron not indicated unless confirmed iron-deficient; iron studies to be checked if hemoglobin declines further.

6. Grade 1 nausea on niraparib — managed with PRN ondansetron; bedtime dosing continued. Adequate symptom control at current frequency of use. No weight loss of concern (weight stable at 63.4 kg).

---

PLAN:

1. Niraparib (Zejula): Continue at 100 mg orally once daily at bedtime. No further dose reduction needed at this time given platelets now Grade 1 and clinically stable. Continue weekly CBC monitoring for an additional 2–3 weeks, then transition to monthly CBC monitoring if platelets remain stable at or above 100,000/uL on three consecutive checks. Patient reminded to avoid NSAIDs, aspirin, and activities with significant injury risk given mild thrombocytopenia.

2. Hypertension management: Start amlodipine (Norvasc) 5 mg orally once daily. Patient educated on medication, common side effects (leg edema, flushing), and the importance of consistent use. She is to continue home blood pressure monitoring (goal BP <130/80 mmHg). Will coordinate with her primary care physician regarding ongoing management. Recheck blood pressure in 2–4 weeks; dose to be titrated to 10 mg if inadequate response. Headaches expected to improve with blood pressure control.

3. CA-125 and tumor marker monitoring: CA-125 remains reassuringly normal at 9 U/mL. Repeat CA-125 and HE4 in 3 months at next scheduled visit. No upward trend to date.

4. Surveillance imaging: CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast planned at 12 months from completion of chemotherapy (approximately 4 months from now). No urgent indication for imaging at this time given clinical and biochemical stability.

5. Peripheral neuropathy: Continue duloxetine 30 mg daily. Encourage gentle physical activity (yoga, walking) as tolerated, which patient is already engaged in. Refer to physical therapy for peripheral neuropathy-specific exercises if Grade 1 persists or worsens.

6. Breast cancer risk reduction: Reminder given regarding upcoming annual breast MRI (due in approximately 2 months) and mammogram given germline BRCA1 status. Patient verbalized understanding. Referral to high-risk breast program to be placed if not yet completed. Discussed risk-reducing mastectomy again briefly; patient continues to decline at this time, preferring intensive surveillance. Decision respected and documented.

7. Ongoing psychosocial support: Patient continues to engage with the oncology support group. No new referrals required at this time. Encouraged to continue regular activity and social engagement.

8. Return to clinic: In 8 weeks for CBC recheck (transitioning to monthly monitoring if stable) and general follow-up. Sooner if fever, unexpected bleeding, significant bruising, worsening headache, or any new symptoms of concern.

9. Communication: PCP notified via secure message regarding BP findings, initiation of amlodipine, and ongoing surveillance plan.

Total visit time: 45 minutes, greater than 50% of which was spent in counseling and coordination of care.

Attending Physician: [Medical Oncology Attending]
Division of Medical Oncology / Gynecologic Oncology
